texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Olefin/paraffin gas separations with 6FDA-based polyimide membranes
texts.text: Olefin/paraffin gas separations with 6FDA-based polyimide membranes

texts.label: text

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas permeation and sorption experiments were carried out for the gases ethylene, ethane, propylene and propane using polyimides based on 4,4 0 -(hexafluoroisopropylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA). Composite membranes and free films were used. Experiments were performed at 308 K and feed pressures up to 17 atm for ethylene and ethane and 9 atm for propylene and propane. Mixed gas permeation experiments were carried out with 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin feed mixtures. For all investigated polyimides, the ideal ethylene/ethane separation factor ranged between 3.3 and 4.4 and the ideal propylene/propane separation factor ranged between 10 and 16 at a feed pressure of 3.8 atm and 308 K. In mixed gas permeation experiments, up to 20% lower selectivity was found for the ethylene/ethane separation and up to 50% reduced selectivity for the propylene/propane separation compared to the ideal selectivity. The influence of feed temperature on separation and permeation properties will be discussed based on pure gas permeability data at 298 and 308 K. © 2000 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.
texts.text: Pure gas permeation and sorption experiments were carried out for the gases ethylene, ethane, propylene and propane using polyimides based on 4,4 0 -(hexafluoroisopropylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA). Composite membranes and free films were used. Experiments were performed at 308 K and feed pressures up to 17 atm for ethylene and ethane and 9 atm for propylene and propane. Mixed gas permeation experiments were carried out with 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin feed mixtures. For all investigated polyimides, the ideal ethylene/ethane separation factor ranged between 3.3 and 4.4 and the ideal propylene/propane separation factor ranged between 10 and 16 at a feed pressure of 3.8 atm and 308 K. In mixed gas permeation experiments, up to 20% lower selectivity was found for the ethylene/ethane separation and up to 50% reduced selectivity for the propylene/propane separation compared to the ideal selectivity. The influence of feed temperature on separation and permeation properties will be discussed based on pure gas permeability data at 298 and 308 K. © 2000 Elsevier Science B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The olefins ethylene and propylene are highly important synthetic chemicals in terms of volume, sales value and number of derivatives. Worldwide ethylene production was 65 million tons per year in 1995. An additional production increase of 15% is expected in the next 5 years [1]. Large quantities of ethylene and propylene are used as feedstock in the production of plastics such as polyethylene or polypropylene. The most widely used production process for olefins is the cracking of C4-hydrocarbon fractions, followed
texts.text: The olefins ethylene and propylene are highly important synthetic chemicals in terms of volume, sales value and number of derivatives. Worldwide ethylene production was 65 million tons per year in 1995. An additional production increase of 15% is expected in the next 5 years [1]. Large quantities of ethylene and propylene are used as feedstock in the production of plastics such as polyethylene or polypropylene. The most widely used production process for olefins is the cracking of C4-hydrocarbon fractions, followed

texts.label: text
texts.orig: by a dehydrogenation reaction. The typical conversion equilibrium of the dehydrogenation reaction is around 50-60%. The olefin/paraffin mixture obtained is difficult to separate because of the similar physical properties of the saturated/unsaturated hydrocarbons, as shown in Table 1 [2,3]. The separation of olefin/paraffin mixtures is currently carried out by energy intensive low temperature distillations as shown in Fig. 1. Huge splitter columns are necessary to separate the unsaturated/saturated mixtures, i.e. a splitter with 120 trays is used to obtain ethylene in a purity sufficient for polymerization reactions. Of course, it is of great interest to develop new energy saving processes to separate olefin/paraffin mixtures.

Huge splitter columns are necessary to separate the unsaturated/saturated mixtures, i.e. a splitter with 120 trays is used to obtain ethylene in a purity sufficient for polymerization reactions. Of course, it is of great interest to develop new energy saving processes to separate olefin/paraffin mixtures.

A possible reduction of the splitter column might be of highest interest because the olefin/paraffin separation train is a more than half of the total cost of an olefin plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many studies of facilitated transport using ion exchange membranes, containing silver ions as a complexing agent, have been published [7-15]. Membranes with facilitated transport properties indeed show very good selectivity and relatively high permeability coefficients for the olefins, but the separation process typically has to be carried out under saturated water vapor to ensure the transport of silver/olefin complexes through the membrane, adding considerable complications.
texts.text: Many studies of facilitated transport using ion exchange membranes, containing silver ions as a complexing agent, have been published [7-15]. Membranes with facilitated transport properties indeed show very good selectivity and relatively high permeability coefficients for the olefins, but the separation process typically has to be carried out under saturated water vapor to ensure the transport of silver/olefin complexes through the membrane, adding considerable complications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The separation of olefin/paraffin mixtures through various polymeric membranes without carriers has been studied recently. Silicone rubber, polysulfone, cellulose acetate, PDMS, 1,2-polybutadiene and polyethylene seem to be useless as membrane materials for the olefin/paraffin separation. Ideal propylene/propane separation factors of 1 for silicon rubber and 3.8 for cellulose acetate at 313 K were found [16]. For PDMS and for 1,2-polybutadiene, ideal propylene/propane separation factors of 1.1 and 1.7, respectively, were reported at 323 K [17]. For polyethylene as membrane material also, a rather low ideal selectivity of 1.6 was found [18].
texts.text: The separation of olefin/paraffin mixtures through various polymeric membranes without carriers has been studied recently. Silicone rubber, polysulfone, cellulose acetate, PDMS, 1,2-polybutadiene and polyethylene seem to be useless as membrane materials for the olefin/paraffin separation. Ideal propylene/propane separation factors of 1 for silicon rubber and 3.8 for cellulose acetate at 313 K were found [16]. For PDMS and for 1,2-polybutadiene, ideal propylene/propane separation factors of 1.1 and 1.7, respectively, were reported at 323 K [17]. For polyethylene as membrane material also, a rather low ideal selectivity of 1.6 was found [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More suitable as membrane material for the olefin/paraffin separation seems to be PPO, which shows the ideal propylene/propane selectivity of 9.1 at 323 K with a propylene permeability of 2.3 barrer [17]. Currently, the most promising membrane materials for the separation of olefins/paraffins seem to be the polyimides. With carbonized polyimides, very high propylene/propane selectivities up to 56 have been found. [19]. Recently, it has been reported that ideal propylene/propane separation factors of up to 77 could be reached with fluorine containing polyimides [18].
texts.text: More suitable as membrane material for the olefin/paraffin separation seems to be PPO, which shows the ideal propylene/propane selectivity of 9.1 at 323 K with a propylene permeability of 2.3 barrer [17]. Currently, the most promising membrane materials for the separation of olefins/paraffins seem to be the polyimides. With carbonized polyimides, very high propylene/propane selectivities up to 56 have been found. [19]. Recently, it has been reported that ideal propylene/propane separation factors of up to 77 could be reached with fluorine containing polyimides [18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the present work, different 4,4 0 -(hexafluoroisoproylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA)-based polyimides were tested concerning the permeation and sorption properties of pure ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene and for determining separation factors for 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin feed mixtures. Possible plasticization effects were investigated at higher feed pressure, because this might reduce the separation characteristics dramatically. The influence of feed temperature on separation and permeation properties will be discussed based on pure gas permeability data at 298 and 308 K.
texts.text: In the present work, different 4,4 0 -(hexafluoroisoproylidene) diphthalic anhydride (6FDA)-based polyimides were tested concerning the permeation and sorption properties of pure ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene and for determining separation factors for 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin feed mixtures. Possible plasticization effects were investigated at higher feed pressure, because this might reduce the separation characteristics dramatically. The influence of feed temperature on separation and permeation properties will be discussed based on pure gas permeability data at 298 and 308 K.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Background and theory
texts.text: 2. Background and theory

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permeation of a penetrant through polymer membranes for gas separations is characterized by a permeability coefficient P , which is generally defined as the flux N normalized by pressure and membrane thickness:
texts.text: The permeation of a penetrant through polymer membranes for gas separations is characterized by a permeability coefficient P , which is generally defined as the flux N normalized by pressure and membrane thickness:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Eq. (1), p 2 and p 1 are upstream and downstream pressures of the penetrants, respectively, and ' is the membrane thickness. In these materials, permeation is a solution/diffusion process, and the permeability coefficient can be expressed as a product of an average diffusion coefficient D and a solubility coefficient S [20]:
texts.text: In Eq. (1), p 2 and p 1 are upstream and downstream pressures of the penetrants, respectively, and ' is the membrane thickness. In these materials, permeation is a solution/diffusion process, and the permeability coefficient can be expressed as a product of an average diffusion coefficient D and a solubility coefficient S [20]:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: P D S D (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The solubility coefficient S is determined by the condensibility of the penetrants, by the polymer/penetrant interactions and by the amount of free volume existing in the glassy polymer. The average diffusion coefficient D is a measure of the mobility of the penetrants between the upstream and downstream conditions in the membrane. The diffusion coefficient D depends on packing and motion of the polymer segments and on the size and shape of the penetrating molecule.
texts.text: The solubility coefficient S is determined by the condensibility of the penetrants, by the polymer/penetrant interactions and by the amount of free volume existing in the glassy polymer. The average diffusion coefficient D is a measure of the mobility of the penetrants between the upstream and downstream conditions in the membrane. The diffusion coefficient D depends on packing and motion of the polymer segments and on the size and shape of the penetrating molecule.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The permselectivity of a membrane for gas separations is characterized by a separation factor A = B defined in terms of the downstream ( y ) and upstream ( x ) mole fractions of the two components A and B in Eq. (3):
texts.text: The permselectivity of a membrane for gas separations is characterized by a separation factor A = B defined in terms of the downstream ( y ) and upstream ( x ) mole fractions of the two components A and B in Eq. (3):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: A = B D y A =y B x A =x B (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In cases where the downstream pressure is negligible, the separation factor A = B is equal to the ratio of permeabilities of the two gases A and B under mixed gas feed conditions as shown in Eq. (4):
texts.text: In cases where the downstream pressure is negligible, the separation factor A = B is equal to the ratio of permeabilities of the two gases A and B under mixed gas feed conditions as shown in Eq. (4):

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: A = B D P A P B (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ideal separation factor A = B is a measure of the intrinsic permselectivity of a membrane material for mixtures of A and B. By substituting Eq. (2) in Eq. (4), the ideal separation factor can be split into two parts:
texts.text: The ideal separation factor A = B is a measure of the intrinsic permselectivity of a membrane material for mixtures of A and B. By substituting Eq. (2) in Eq. (4), the ideal separation factor can be split into two parts:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: A = B D D A D B S A S B (5)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where D A/ D B is the diffusivity selectivity and S A/ S B is the solubility selectivity.
texts.text: where D A/ D B is the diffusivity selectivity and S A/ S B is the solubility selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: synthesized by a polycondensation reaction of dianhydrides and diamines in dimethyl acetamide followed by a chemical imidization with a triethylamine/acetic anhydride mixture, described in detail in [21]. For all polyimides, free film membrane samples were obtained by casting a filtered 2-5 wt.% solution of polyimide in methylene chloride onto a glass plate. After evaporation of the solvent, the film samples were stripped off by immersion of the plate in water followed by drying under vacuum at 523 K overnight. Composite membranes were prepared by casting a filtered 0.4-0.5 wt.% polyimide/methylene chloride solution onto a porous Anopore ® disc (Whatman).
texts.text: synthesized by a polycondensation reaction of dianhydrides and diamines in dimethyl acetamide followed by a chemical imidization with a triethylamine/acetic anhydride mixture, described in detail in [21]. For all polyimides, free film membrane samples were obtained by casting a filtered 2-5 wt.% solution of polyimide in methylene chloride onto a glass plate. After evaporation of the solvent, the film samples were stripped off by immersion of the plate in water followed by drying under vacuum at 523 K overnight. Composite membranes were prepared by casting a filtered 0.4-0.5 wt.% polyimide/methylene chloride solution onto a porous Anopore ® disc (Whatman).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Apparatus
texts.text: 3.2. Apparatus

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas permeability measurements were performed with ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene up to a feed pressure of 17 atm for ethylene and ethane and up to 9 atm for propane and propylene. For the permeation experiments, standard permeation techniques, described elsewhere in detail, were used [26]. Thereby, the effective membrane area was 13 cm 2 and the permeate volume was 998.5 cm 3 . The leak rates, which were determined with aluminium foil, were 1.5 10 -4 and 2.5 10 -4 Torr/min, respectively, for the two different permeation cells used. All gases showed a minimum GC purity of 99% (Wilson Oxygen). The permeation measurements for each single gas were carried out with different membrane samples to exclude possible errors caused by conditioning or plasticization effects.
texts.text: Pure gas permeability measurements were performed with ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene up to a feed pressure of 17 atm for ethylene and ethane and up to 9 atm for propane and propylene. For the permeation experiments, standard permeation techniques, described elsewhere in detail, were used [26]. Thereby, the effective membrane area was 13 cm 2 and the permeate volume was 998.5 cm 3 . The leak rates, which were determined with aluminium foil, were 1.5 10 -4 and 2.5 10 -4 Torr/min, respectively, for the two different permeation cells used. All gases showed a minimum GC purity of 99% (Wilson Oxygen). The permeation measurements for each single gas were carried out with different membrane samples to exclude possible errors caused by conditioning or plasticization effects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For mixed gas permeation experiments, 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin mixtures were used. Feed and permeate gas samples were analyzed by gas chromatography, using a carboxen 1000 (15 ft 1/8 in.) column. The selectivity was calculated by Eq. (3).
texts.text: For mixed gas permeation experiments, 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin mixtures were used. Feed and permeate gas samples were analyzed by gas chromatography, using a carboxen 1000 (15 ft 1/8 in.) column. The selectivity was calculated by Eq. (3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas sorption measurements for ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene up to 8.3 atm were carried out using a pressure decay sorption cell as well as a magnetic microbalance, described in [27,28], respectively.
texts.text: Pure gas sorption measurements for ethane, ethylene, propane and propylene up to 8.3 atm were carried out using a pressure decay sorption cell as well as a magnetic microbalance, described in [27,28], respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Results and discussion
texts.text: 4. Results and discussion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 3 shows the ideal ethylene/ethane and ideal propylene/propane selectivity as well as ethylene and propylene permeability at 3.8 atm feed pressure and 308 K for different 6FDA polyimide membrane samples. For all polyimides investigated, a preferred permeability of olefins compared to paraffins was found because of the smaller molecular dimensions ( LJ) of the olefins. The olefins show a shorter C-C distance compared to the paraffins (single bond D 1.534 Å, double bond D 1.337 Å) and due to the sp 2 hybridization of the carbon atoms, the olefins are rigid 'flat molecules' compared to the paraffins with sp 3 hybridization, where free C-C rotation is possible, as shown in Fig. 3. The permeability of hydrocarbons in

The olefins show a shorter C-C distance compared to the paraffins (single bond D 1.534 Å, double bond D 1.337 Å) and due to the sp 2 hybridization of the carbon atoms, the olefins are rigid 'flat molecules' compared to the paraffins with sp 3 hybridization, where free C-C rotation is possible, as shown in Fig. 3. texts.text: polyimides strongly depends on the shape of the penetrating molecules, which is obvious by comparing the pure gas permeabilities of methane and ethylene. texts.label: text
texts.orig: But the permeability of olefins in 6FDA polyimides also depends on the polymer structure, i.e. the free volume. For the 6FDA-6FpDA, seven times higher olefin permeabilities were found, compared to the 6FDA-mPD, as shown in Table 3. Generally, for the C2 separation, ideal selectivities of 3.3-4.4 are obtained. On the other hand, for the C3 separation, ideal selectivities of 10 to 16 were found. The best results for selectivity as well as olefin permeability, possibly sufficient for commercial application, have been reached with the 6FDA-6FpDA. Therefore, pure and mixed gas permeation behavior as well as sorption properties have been studied for olefin/paraffin separation with the 6FDA-6FpDA in detail.
texts.text: But the permeability of olefins in 6FDA polyimides also depends on the polymer structure, i.e. the free volume. For the 6FDA-6FpDA, seven times higher olefin permeabilities were found, compared to the 6FDA-mPD, as shown in Table 3. Generally, for the C2 separation, ideal selectivities of 3.3-4.4 are obtained. On the other hand, for the C3 separation, ideal selectivities of 10 to 16 were found. The best results for selectivity as well as olefin permeability, possibly sufficient for commercial application, have been reached with the 6FDA-6FpDA. Therefore, pure and mixed gas permeation behavior as well as sorption properties have been studied for olefin/paraffin separation with the 6FDA-6FpDA in detail.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1. Pure gas permeation experiments
texts.text: 4.1. Pure gas permeation experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas permeation measurements were carried out for ethylene, ethane, propylene and propane using free films as well as composite membranes. The thickness of films was measured with a micrometer at 15-20 points on each film sample. The average values showed a maximum deviation of C / -10% to the highest and the lowest value of the measured thickness. The thickness of the composites was determined by measuring the P / ' for O2 and N2 and calculating the thickness using the permeability data obtained with 20-30 m m thick film samples. In most cases, the ideal O2/N2 selectivity was slightly lower for the composites ( O2 = N2 D 4.4-4.56) than for free films ( O2 = N2 D 4.7). Therefore, the thickness calculated using the P / ' for O2 was slightly higher than the thickness calculated by the P / ' for N2. The average thickness showed maximum deviation of 4-5%. As shown in Figs. 4 and 5, the results for permeability as well as selectivity data are very close using film and composite samples.
texts.text: Pure gas permeation measurements were carried out for ethylene, ethane, propylene and propane using free films as well as composite membranes. The thickness of films was measured with a micrometer at 15-20 points on each film sample. The average values showed a maximum deviation of C / -10% to the highest and the lowest value of the measured thickness. The thickness of the composites was determined by measuring the P / ' for O2 and N2 and calculating the thickness using the permeability data obtained with 20-30 m m thick film samples. In most cases, the ideal O2/N2 selectivity was slightly lower for the composites ( O2 = N2 D 4.4-4.56) than for free films ( O2 = N2 D 4.7). Therefore, the thickness calculated using the P / ' for O2 was slightly higher than the thickness calculated by the P / ' for N2. The average thickness showed maximum deviation of 4-5%. As shown in Figs. 4 and 5, the results for permeability as well as selectivity data are very close using film and composite samples.

texts.orig: Fig. 4. Pure ethane permeability ( d D 6.88 m m film, s D 2.88 m m composite at 2 atm and 4.95 m m composite at 3.8 atm feed pressure) and ethylene permeability ( j D 7.74 m m film; h D 2.88 m m composite at 2 atm and 4.95 m m composite at 3.8 atm feed pressure). All experiments were performed with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide at 308 K.
texts.text: Fig. 4. Pure ethane permeability ( d D 6.88 m m film, s D 2.88 m m composite at 2 atm and 4.95 m m composite at 3.8 atm feed pressure) and ethylene permeability ( j D 7.74 m m film; h D 2.88 m m composite at 2 atm and 4.95 m m composite at 3.8 atm feed pressure). All experiments were performed with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide at 308 K.

texts.orig: Fig. 5. Pure propane permeability ( d D 2 m m composite, s D 10 m m film) and propylene permeability ( j D 4.88 m m composite, h D 10 m m film). All experiments were performed with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide at 308 K.
texts.text: Fig. 5. Pure propane permeability ( d D 2 m m composite, s D 10 m m film) and propylene permeability ( j D 4.88 m m composite, h D 10 m m film). All experiments were performed with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide at 308 K.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Fig. 4, the results for the permeation of ethylene and ethane for the 6FDA-6FpDA at 35 C are shown. Ideal selectivites at 2 and 3.8 atm feed pressure were determined by measuring the ethylene and the ethane permeability of two different composites, 2.88 and 4.95 m m, respectively. In order to investigate possible plasticization effects, permeation experiments for each gas component were carried out with different film samples (7.74 and 6.88 m m). Before and after the hydrocarbon exposure, ideal O2/N2 selectivity for the film samples was determined. As Fig. 4 shows, with ethylene and ethane, no plasticization effects occurred, and the O2/N2 selectivity, measured before and after hydrocarbon permeation, did not change. Also, O2 and N2 permeabilities did not change significantly after C2 exposure. Ethylene as well as ethane permeability decreases with increasing feed pressure. Decreasing permeability with increasing feed pressure seen for glassy materials is consistent with the well known 'dual mode sorption' and transport model predictions. The effect of decreasing permeability with increasing feed pressure is stronger with ethylene compared to ethane. Therefore, the ideal selectivity will decrease slightly with increasing feed pressure.
texts.text: In Fig. 4, the results for the permeation of ethylene and ethane for the 6FDA-6FpDA at 35 C are shown. Ideal selectivites at 2 and 3.8 atm feed pressure were determined by measuring the ethylene and the ethane permeability of two different composites, 2.88 and 4.95 m m, respectively. In order to investigate possible plasticization effects, permeation experiments for each gas component were carried out with different film samples (7.74 and 6.88 m m). Before and after the hydrocarbon exposure, ideal O2/N2 selectivity for the film samples was determined. As Fig. 4 shows, with ethylene and ethane, no plasticization effects occurred, and the O2/N2 selectivity, measured before and after hydrocarbon permeation, did not change. Also, O2 and N2 permeabilities did not change significantly after C2 exposure. Ethylene as well as ethane permeability decreases with increasing feed pressure. Decreasing permeability with increasing feed pressure seen for glassy materials is consistent with the well known 'dual mode sorption' and transport model predictions. The effect of decreasing permeability with increasing feed pressure is stronger with ethylene compared to ethane. Therefore, the ideal selectivity will decrease slightly with increasing feed pressure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Most of the propylene/propane permeation measurements were carried out with thin composite membranes because of the extremely low permeability of propane. It was found that the pressure increase due to permeating propane will be in the same magnitude as the leak rate, if membranes with thickness much higher than 10 m m were used.
texts.text: Most of the propylene/propane permeation measurements were carried out with thin composite membranes because of the extremely low permeability of propane. It was found that the pressure increase due to permeating propane will be in the same magnitude as the leak rate, if membranes with thickness much higher than 10 m m were used.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows all data for pure propylene and propane permeability at 308 K measured for different composite and film samples of the 6FDA-6FpDA. Ideal selectivities at 2 and 3.8 atm feed pressure were determined by measuring propylene and propane permeability of two different composites, 2 and 4.88 m m, respectively. The ideal selectivity was also determined by measuring propylene and propane permeability with a 10 m m thick film sample. Permeability and ideal selectivity obtained with composite and film show no significant difference.
texts.text: Fig. 5 shows all data for pure propylene and propane permeability at 308 K measured for different composite and film samples of the 6FDA-6FpDA. Ideal selectivities at 2 and 3.8 atm feed pressure were determined by measuring propylene and propane permeability of two different composites, 2 and 4.88 m m, respectively. The ideal selectivity was also determined by measuring propylene and propane permeability with a 10 m m thick film sample. Permeability and ideal selectivity obtained with composite and film show no significant difference.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to investigate possible plasticization effects, permeation experiments for propylene and propane were carried out with two different composite samples (4.88 and 2 m m) determining the O2/N2 selectivity before and after hydrocarbon exposure.
texts.text: In order to investigate possible plasticization effects, permeation experiments for propylene and propane were carried out with two different composite samples (4.88 and 2 m m) determining the O2/N2 selectivity before and after hydrocarbon exposure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With increasing feed pressure, a strong increase in permeability for propylene as well as for propane was found due to plasticization. The ideal selectivity remains constant with increasing feed pressure because the plasticization effect for both of the pure C3 hydrocarbons, the saturated and the unsaturated ones, seems to be similar. The plasticization is due to high solubility of the hydrocarbons in the polyimides and commonly leads to a drastic loss in selectivity. Before the exposure of C3 hydrocarbons, the composites show O2/N2 selectivity of 4.4 and 4.6, respectively. After the composites were exposed to C3 hydrocarbons, O2/N2 selectivity decreased to 3.8 and 3.4.
texts.text: With increasing feed pressure, a strong increase in permeability for propylene as well as for propane was found due to plasticization. The ideal selectivity remains constant with increasing feed pressure because the plasticization effect for both of the pure C3 hydrocarbons, the saturated and the unsaturated ones, seems to be similar. The plasticization is due to high solubility of the hydrocarbons in the polyimides and commonly leads to a drastic loss in selectivity. Before the exposure of C3 hydrocarbons, the composites show O2/N2 selectivity of 4.4 and 4.6, respectively. After the composites were exposed to C3 hydrocarbons, O2/N2 selectivity decreased to 3.8 and 3.4.

texts.text: 4.2. Sorption experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figs. 6 and 7 show the sorption isotherms for the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide with ethylene and ethane and propylene and propane, respectively, at 308 K. As mentioned before, two different methods were used for determining the sorption of hydrocarbons in the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide. The average deviation between the data obtained with the pressure decay method and the data measured with a magnetic microbalance is in the range of the experimental error for sorption experiments. As Fig. 6 shows, very small differences in solubility between ethylene and ethane are observed. With increasing feed pressure, the difference in solubility increases slightly. For propylene/propane sorption, the difference in solubil-

The average deviation between the data obtained with the pressure decay method and the data measured with a magnetic microbalance is in the range of the experimental error for sorption experiments. As Fig. 6 shows, very small differences in solubility between ethylene and ethane are observed. With increasing feed pressure, the difference in solubility increases slightly. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Generally, it can be concluded that, with polyimides as membrane materials, olefins are sorbed preferentially, which is in good agreement with [17,18]. The reason for this is that the olefins show higher polarizability due to their p -electron system, compared to the paraffins. But the difference in solubility is so small that the olefin/paraffin separation process is mainly controlled by the diffusivity selectivity.
texts.text: Generally, it can be concluded that, with polyimides as membrane materials, olefins are sorbed preferentially, which is in good agreement with [17,18]. The reason for this is that the olefins show higher polarizability due to their p -electron system, compared to the paraffins. But the difference in solubility is so small that the olefin/paraffin separation process is mainly controlled by the diffusivity selectivity.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Mixed gas experiments
texts.text: 4.3. Mixed gas experiments

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixed gas experiments were conducted with a 50 : 50 ethylene/ethane feed mixture at 308 K. As shown in Fig. 8, the selectivity for ethylene/ethane remained constant at 3.2 with increasing ethylene feed pressure for the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide. The selectivity for a mixed gas feed is around 20% lower than the ideal selectivity calculated with Eq. (3) based on the pure gas permeation data.
texts.text: Mixed gas experiments were conducted with a 50 : 50 ethylene/ethane feed mixture at 308 K. As shown in Fig. 8, the selectivity for ethylene/ethane remained constant at 3.2 with increasing ethylene feed pressure for the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide. The selectivity for a mixed gas feed is around 20% lower than the ideal selectivity calculated with Eq. (3) based on the pure gas permeation data.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Table 5, the total permeabilities as well as the individual olefin permeabilities obtained in mixed gas experiments at different feed pressures are listed. For the ethylene/ethane separation, a strongly reduced permeability for ethylene as well as for ethane was observed. This is consistent with the predictions of the dual mode sorption and transport model. Due to the stronger reduction of ethylene permeability compared to ethane permeability, a 15-20% lower selectivity compared to the ideal selectivity was found. The total permeability decreased slightly with increasing feed pressure as expected by pure gas permeation data for ethylene and ethane.
texts.text: In Table 5, the total permeabilities as well as the individual olefin permeabilities obtained in mixed gas experiments at different feed pressures are listed. For the ethylene/ethane separation, a strongly reduced permeability for ethylene as well as for ethane was observed. This is consistent with the predictions of the dual mode sorption and transport model. Due to the stronger reduction of ethylene permeability compared to ethane permeability, a 15-20% lower selectivity compared to the ideal selectivity was found. The total permeability decreased slightly with increasing feed pressure as expected by pure gas permeation data for ethylene and ethane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the propylene/propane separation, the total permeability in mixed gas experiments increased with increasing feed pressure. This was expected from pure gas permeation data. Due to the stronger increase of partial propane permeability compared to the propylene permeability, loss in selectivity is observed.
texts.text: For the propylene/propane separation, the total permeability in mixed gas experiments increased with increasing feed pressure. This was expected from pure gas permeation data. Due to the stronger increase of partial propane permeability compared to the propylene permeability, loss in selectivity is observed.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Temperature effects on olefin/paraffin separations
texts.text: 4.4. Temperature effects on olefin/paraffin separations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Pure gas permeability as well as ideal permselectivity for the 6FDA-6FpDA at 298 and 308 K and a feed pressure of 2 atm were investigated. For the ethylene/ethane separation, with increasing temperature, increasing permeability as well as increasing ideal selectivity were found. For example, an ideal selectivity of 3.13 combined with an ethylene permeability of 1.5 barrer was obtained at 298 K, whereas the ideal selectivity at 308 K was 4.6 with an ethylene permeability of 2.5 barrer.
texts.text: Pure gas permeability as well as ideal permselectivity for the 6FDA-6FpDA at 298 and 308 K and a feed pressure of 2 atm were investigated. For the ethylene/ethane separation, with increasing temperature, increasing permeability as well as increasing ideal selectivity were found. For example, an ideal selectivity of 3.13 combined with an ethylene permeability of 1.5 barrer was obtained at 298 K, whereas the ideal selectivity at 308 K was 4.6 with an ethylene permeability of 2.5 barrer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For the propylene/propane separation, a reduction of the feed temperature by 10 K leads to a significant increase in selectivity without a change in permeability as shown in Table 6. For example, at 308 K, an ideal selectivity of 16 was observed, whereas at 298 K, an ideal selectivity of 32.9 was reached, which is in good agreement with [29]. An ideal propylene/propane selectivity of up to 77 at 298 K, as reported in [18], could not be reached in our work. The reason for this discrepancy is not clear. Initially, it was thought that the thin composite layers tested in our work differed from the 20 m mfilm sample in [18] and might have in-
texts.text: For the propylene/propane separation, a reduction of the feed temperature by 10 K leads to a significant increase in selectivity without a change in permeability as shown in Table 6. For example, at 308 K, an ideal selectivity of 16 was observed, whereas at 298 K, an ideal selectivity of 32.9 was reached, which is in good agreement with [29]. An ideal propylene/propane selectivity of up to 77 at 298 K, as reported in [18], could not be reached in our work. The reason for this discrepancy is not clear. Initially, it was thought that the thin composite layers tested in our work differed from the 20 m mfilm sample in [18] and might have in-

texts.label: text
texts.orig: troduced artifacts [30]; however, rechecking our composite data with 10 m m dense films revealed selectivities of 15 at 308 K, which is in good agreement with our composite values of 16. As mentioned before, with our equipment, permeability measurements with membranes much thicker than 10 m m were not possible due to the fact that especially in the lower temperature range, the pressure increase caused by permeating propane is so small that it reaches the same magnitude as the leak rate of the apparatus. Another possibility for the discrepancy might be differences in the polymer preparation. In our work, dimethylacetamide was used as a solvent, whereas N -methylpyrrolidone was used in [18]. Furthermore, for the membrane formation, dichloromethane was used in our work, while diethyleneglycol dimethylether was used in [18]. For comparison, all temperature dependent propylene permeability data as well as ideal propylene/propane selectivities are listed in Table 6.
texts.text: troduced artifacts [30]; however, rechecking our composite data with 10 m m dense films revealed selectivities of 15 at 308 K, which is in good agreement with our composite values of 16. As mentioned before, with our equipment, permeability measurements with membranes much thicker than 10 m m were not possible due to the fact that especially in the lower temperature range, the pressure increase caused by permeating propane is so small that it reaches the same magnitude as the leak rate of the apparatus. Another possibility for the discrepancy might be differences in the polymer preparation. In our work, dimethylacetamide was used as a solvent, whereas N -methylpyrrolidone was used in [18]. Furthermore, for the membrane formation, dichloromethane was used in our work, while diethyleneglycol dimethylether was used in [18]. For comparison, all temperature dependent propylene permeability data as well as ideal propylene/propane selectivities are listed in Table 6.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polyimides, especially 6FDA-based ones, are very promising materials for membrane-based separation of olefin/paraffin mixtures. Permeability and selectivity strongly depends upon the diamine monomer used in the polymerization reaction. The best results for selectivity as well as permeability have been obtained with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide.
texts.text: Polyimides, especially 6FDA-based ones, are very promising materials for membrane-based separation of olefin/paraffin mixtures. Permeability and selectivity strongly depends upon the diamine monomer used in the polymerization reaction. The best results for selectivity as well as permeability have been obtained with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The differences in solubility for the olefin/paraffins are very small. Therefore, the preferred permeation of olefins compared to paraffins is due to their preferential diffusion, which is based on the smaller molecular size and flat shape. With C2 hydrocarbons, saturated and unsaturated, no plasticization effect was found. Unfortunately, with C3 hydrocarbons, very strong plasticization effects, starting at very low feed pressures, were observed. In 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin mixed gas experiments at 308 K with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide for C2 separation, a constant selectivity of 3.2 was found, which is around 20% lower than the ideal selectivity. For C3 separation, a decreasing selectivity with increasing feed pressure was reached, which was finally up to 50% lower than the ideal selectivity, determined by pure gas permeation experiments.
texts.text: The differences in solubility for the olefin/paraffins are very small. Therefore, the preferred permeation of olefins compared to paraffins is due to their preferential diffusion, which is based on the smaller molecular size and flat shape. With C2 hydrocarbons, saturated and unsaturated, no plasticization effect was found. Unfortunately, with C3 hydrocarbons, very strong plasticization effects, starting at very low feed pressures, were observed. In 50 : 50 olefin/paraffin mixed gas experiments at 308 K with the 6FDA-6FpDA polyimide for C2 separation, a constant selectivity of 3.2 was found, which is around 20% lower than the ideal selectivity. For C3 separation, a decreasing selectivity with increasing feed pressure was reached, which was finally up to 50% lower than the ideal selectivity, determined by pure gas permeation experiments.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For further development of new membrane materials for the olefin/paraffin separation, polyimides with a high free volume, combined with a rigid, inflexible polymer structure, possibly with cross-linking units included, have to be synthesized to obtain high flux, high selectivity and swelling resistant membranes materials.
texts.text: For further development of new membrane materials for the olefin/paraffin separation, polyimides with a high free volume, combined with a rigid, inflexible polymer structure, possibly with cross-linking units included, have to be synthesized to obtain high flux, high selectivity and swelling resistant membranes materials.
